name: California
metaTitle: California Personal Injury Law — Deadlines & Legal Rights
metaDescription: California personal injury laws, statute of limitations, fault rules, and insurance minimums. Educational guide for accident victims in CA.

statuteOfLimitations.personalInjury: 2 years from the date of injury (CCP § 335.1)
statuteOfLimitations.propertyDamage: 3 years from the date of damage (CCP § 338)
statuteOfLimitations.wrongfulDeath: 2 years from the date of death (CCP § 335.1)

faultRule.description: California follows pure comparative fault (Li v. Yellow Cab Co., 1975). An injured person can recover damages even if they were 99% at fault, but their recovery is reduced by their percentage of fault. There is no bar to recovery based on the injured party's degree of fault.

reportingDeadlines.details: If a car accident results in injury, death, or property damage over $1,000, it must be reported to the DMV within 10 days using the SR-1 form.
Claims against California state or local government entities must be filed using the Government Claims Program within 6 months of the incident. Failure to comply is a hard bar to recovery.

reportingDeadlines.deadline: Within 30 days to employer; 1 year to file
reportingDeadlines.details: Report the workplace injury to your employer within 30 days. File a formal workers' compensation claim (DWC-1) within 1 year of the injury date.
Review your policy. Most UM/UIM claims must be reported promptly. A lawsuit must be filed within 2 years under California law.

insuranceMinimums.uninsuredMotorist: Must be offered; may be waived in writing

keyLaws.name: Pure Comparative Fault (Li v. Yellow Cab, 1975)
keyLaws.description: California abolished contributory negligence in favor of pure comparative fault, allowing recovery even when the plaintiff is primarily at fault, with damages reduced proportionally.

keyLaws.name: MICRA (Medical Injury Compensation Reform Act)
keyLaws.description: California caps non-economic damages in medical malpractice cases at $350,000 for injuries and $500,000 for wrongful death (as of 2023, with annual increases). Does not apply to general personal injury cases.

keyLaws.name: Proposition 213 — Uninsured Motorist Limitations
keyLaws.description: Drivers operating a vehicle without insurance at the time of an accident may not recover non-economic damages (pain and suffering) even if the other driver was at fault.

keyLaws.name: Government Claims Act (Gov. Code § 810 et seq.)
keyLaws.description: Before suing a California government entity, a formal claim must be filed within 6 months of the incident. Strict compliance is required — courts have limited ability to excuse late filings.

keyLaws.name: California Labor Code § 3600 — Workers' Compensation Exclusivity
keyLaws.description: Workers' compensation is generally the exclusive remedy against an employer for workplace injuries. Third-party civil claims remain available against non-employer defendants.
